q: What does Active Directory Domain Services (AD DS) primarily provide?
o: Centralised authentication and directory services for Windows networks
exp: AD DS stores directory data and manages communication between users and domains, enabling SSO across Windows environments.

q: What is a domain controller (DC)?
o: Server running AD DS and authenticating users in the domain
A file server with access control
exp: Domain controllers hold a copy of the AD database and handle authentication and policy enforcement.

q: What protocol does Active Directory use for authentication?
exp: Kerberos is the default AD authentication protocol; NTLM is used as a legacy fallback.

q: What is a Group Policy Object (GPO)?
o: Configuration settings applied to users and computers in AD
exp: GPOs enforce desktop, security, and software settings across AD-joined machines and user accounts.

q: What is an Organisational Unit (OU) in Active Directory?
o: Container for organising AD objects and applying GPOs
exp: OUs provide administrative delegation and a target for GPO application within a domain.

q: What is an AD forest?
o: The top-level container holding one or more domains sharing a common schema
exp: A forest is the highest-level boundary in AD; all domains in a forest share a schema and Global Catalog.

q: What is LDAP used for in relation to Active Directory?
o: Querying and modifying directory objects over TCP/IP
exp: LDAP (port 389; LDAPS 636) is the protocol used to read and write AD directory information.

q: What is a Kerberos TGT?
o: Ticket-Granting Ticket issued by the KDC after successful initial authentication
exp: The TGT proves the user's identity and is used to request service tickets without re-entering credentials.

q: What is AD replication?
o: Synchronising directory changes across all domain controllers
exp: AD replication keeps all DCs in sync; failures can cause authentication issues and stale data.

q: What is a SID (Security Identifier) in Active Directory?
o: A unique immutable identifier assigned to every AD security principal
exp: SIDs are used internally for access control; renaming an account doesn't change its SID.

q: What is the difference between a security group and a distribution group in AD?
o: Security groups can be used for permissions; distribution groups are for email only
exp: Security groups assign resource permissions; distribution groups are used by Exchange/email systems.

q: What is AD Tiering (the tiered admin model)?
o: Separating admin accounts into tiers to prevent credential theft from spreading
exp: Tier 0 (DCs), Tier 1 (servers), Tier 2 (workstations) prevent Tier 2 compromise leading to Tier 0.

q: What is a trust in Active Directory?
o: A relationship allowing users in one domain/forest to authenticate to another
exp: Trusts can be one-way or two-way, transitive or non-transitive, enabling cross-domain resource access.

q: What is the Global Catalog in AD?
o: A partial replica of all objects in the forest enabling cross-domain searches
exp: The Global Catalog allows users to log on and search for objects across the entire forest.

q: What is an RODC (Read-Only Domain Controller)?
o: A DC that holds a read-only copy of AD, ideal for branch offices
A DC that replicates outbound only
exp: RODCs improve security in low-trust locations by not caching sensitive credentials and not accepting write operations.

q: What is DCSync attack?
o: Mimicking a domain controller to request credential replication from real DCs
exp: DCSync uses DS-Replication-Get-Changes rights to extract password hashes without touching the DC directly.

q: What is the SYSVOL share used for?
o: Distributing GPO files and logon scripts to domain-joined computers
exp: SYSVOL is replicated to all DCs and contains Group Policy files, scripts, and policies.

q: What is Protected Users security group in AD?
o: A group that enforces stricter Kerberos security and disables NTLM for members
A group for password policy only
exp: Members of Protected Users cannot use NTLM, DES, or RC4, and their tickets have shorter lifetimes.

q: What is a Golden Ticket attack?
o: Forging a Kerberos TGT using the KRBTGT account's NTLM hash
exp: With the KRBTGT hash an attacker can forge TGTs for any user with any permissions, persisting even after password resets.

q: What is AdminSDHolder in Active Directory?
o: A container whose ACL is propagated to protected privileged accounts every 60 min
exp: Attackers can achieve persistence by modifying AdminSDHolder's ACL, gaining perpetual control over privileged accounts.

q: What type of service is Okta?
o: Identity-as-a-Service (IDaaS) — cloud-based identity and access management
exp: Okta provides cloud-delivered IAM including SSO, MFA, lifecycle management, and API access management.

q: What is Okta's Universal Directory?
o: A centralised identity store that can sync from multiple sources (AD, HR, LDAP)
A file directory in the cloud
exp: Universal Directory aggregates identities from multiple sources into a single canonical store.

q: What is Okta Verify?
o: Okta's authenticator app providing push notifications and TOTP for MFA
exp: Okta Verify runs on iOS/Android and can use biometrics, push approval, or TOTP for second-factor authentication.

q: What is Okta's sign-on policy used for?
o: Defining which authentication factors are required based on context (app, location, device)
exp: Sign-on policies can require step-up MFA for sensitive apps or from untrusted networks.

q: What is Okta Lifecycle Management?
o: Automating user provisioning and deprovisioning to/from apps via SCIM or Okta APIs
exp: Lifecycle Management ensures users gain access on joining and lose it immediately upon offboarding.

q: What is Okta's app integration network (AIK/OIN)?
o: Catalogue of pre-built integrations for thousands of SaaS applications
exp: The OIN accelerates SSO and provisioning setup for popular apps like Salesforce, Workday, and Google Workspace.

q: What is Okta's Adaptive MFA?
o: Risk-based MFA that adjusts requirements based on context signals like location and device
exp: Adaptive MFA can step up authentication automatically when risk signals (impossible travel, new device) are detected.

q: What is Okta ThreatInsight?
o: Feature that analyses network requests and blocks IPs associated with attack patterns
exp: ThreatInsight uses Okta's network-wide signal intelligence to block or challenge authentication from malicious IPs.

q: What is an Okta group rule?
o: Logic that automatically adds users to groups based on profile attributes
exp: Group rules automate group membership based on profile attributes like department or location.

q: What happened in the 2023 Okta support system breach?
o: Attackers accessed Okta's support case management system and viewed customer tenant data
exp: Threat actors used stolen credentials to access Okta's support tool, viewing HAR files containing session tokens from customers like Cloudflare and 1Password.

q: What is Okta Workflows?
o: No-code automation tool for identity processes like joiner/mover/leaver flows
exp: Okta Workflows uses a flowchart-based interface to automate complex identity processes across apps without custom code.

q: What is Okta's SCIM provisioning used for?
o: Automatically creating, updating, and deactivating user accounts in connected apps
exp: SCIM provisioning keeps downstream app accounts in sync with the Okta identity store in real time.

q: What is Device Trust in Okta?
o: Verifying that a device meets security requirements before granting access
exp: Device Trust uses Okta's MDM integration or device certificates to restrict access to managed devices.

q: What is an Okta application (app) in the Okta admin console?
o: A configured integration representing a service users can access via SSO
An installed app on Okta servers
exp: Each Okta app object stores the SSO configuration (SAML/OIDC), attribute mappings, and assignment rules.

q: What is FastPass in Okta?
o: Passwordless authentication using device-bound cryptographic keys (Okta's FIDO2 implementation)
exp: FastPass uses asymmetric keys stored on the device, verified via Okta Verify, enabling phishing-resistant passwordless access.

q: What is Okta's network zone?
o: A defined IP range or geolocation used in sign-on policies to contextualise access
exp: Network zones classify IP ranges as trusted, blocked, or dynamic, feeding into policy conditions.

q: What is an Okta super admin?
o: The highest-privilege admin role with full control over the Okta org
An admin for a single app only
exp: Super admins should be tightly controlled — Okta recommends break-glass accounts with MFA enforced.

q: How does Okta integrate with Active Directory?
o: Via the Okta AD Agent installed on-premises that syncs users and enables AD-delegated auth
Via direct cloud API to AD
exp: The AD Agent polls AD for changes and enables delegated authentication so users log in with their AD password.

q: What is Okta Access Gateway?
o: On-premises proxy enabling SSO to legacy web apps that don't support modern protocols
exp: Access Gateway extends Okta SSO to header-based and Kerberos/NTLM legacy apps without modifying them.

q: What is Okta's System Log used for?
o: Auditing all events in the Okta org including logins, policy changes, and provisioning
exp: The System Log (via UI or Events API) is the primary audit source for SIEM integration and compliance.

q: What does SAML stand for?
exp: SAML is an XML-based standard for exchanging authentication and authorisation data between parties.

q: In SAML, what is the Identity Provider (IdP)?
o: The system that authenticates the user and issues assertions
exp: The IdP (e.g., Okta, Entra ID) vouches for the user's identity by issuing signed SAML assertions.

q: In SAML, what is the Service Provider (SP)?
o: The application or service consuming SAML assertions to grant access
exp: The SP relies on the IdP's assertion to authenticate users without handling credentials itself.

q: What are the three types of SAML assertions?
o: Authentication, Attribute, and Authorisation Decision assertions
exp: Auth assertions confirm identity; attribute assertions carry user data; authz-decision assertions grant/deny access.

q: What is SP-initiated SAML flow?
o: User accesses SP first; SP redirects to IdP for authentication, then back to SP
exp: SP-initiated flow is most common: SP redirects unauthenticated user to IdP with a SAML AuthnRequest.

q: What is IdP-initiated SAML flow?
o: User starts at the IdP portal and is sent to SP with an unsolicited assertion
exp: In IdP-initiated flow the user clicks an app in the IdP's portal; the IdP sends a Response directly to the SP.

q: What is a SAML binding?
o: The mechanism for transporting SAML messages (e.g., HTTP Redirect, HTTP POST)
exp: Common bindings: HTTP Redirect (AuthnRequest) and HTTP POST (Response with assertion).

q: What is SAML metadata?
o: XML document describing an entity's endpoints, certificates, and supported bindings
exp: Sharing metadata between IdP and SP bootstraps trust without manual certificate exchange.

q: What is a SAML assertion replay attack?
o: Reusing a captured valid SAML assertion to authenticate as the victim
exp: Replay is prevented by short assertion validity windows and SP tracking of assertion IDs.

q: What cryptographic mechanism protects SAML assertions?
o: XML Digital Signatures (and optionally XML Encryption)
exp: SAML assertions are signed with the IdP's private key; the SP verifies using the IdP's public certificate.

q: What is the NameID in SAML?
o: The identifier for the subject (user) in a SAML assertion
exp: NameID formats include emailAddress, persistent, and transient — choice affects privacy and correlation.

q: What is XML Signature Wrapping (XSW) attack in SAML?
o: Manipulating signed XML by adding/moving nodes to make the SP validate an unsigned assertion
exp: XSW exploits XML signature validation by inserting a malicious element while keeping the signature over the original valid element.

q: What is SAML Single Logout (SLO)?
o: Protocol allowing logout from IdP and all connected SPs in one action
Logging out of one SP only
exp: SLO (HTTP Redirect or POST binding) propagates logout to all active sessions across federated apps.

q: What is an EntityID in SAML?
o: A unique identifier for an IdP or SP within a federation
exp: EntityIDs (often URLs) uniquely identify each party; they must match in metadata for trust to work.

q: What attributes are commonly included in SAML assertions?
o: Email, first name, last name, groups/roles — mapped from IdP profile attributes
exp: Attribute statements carry user profile data from IdP to SP, enabling JIT provisioning and RBAC.

q: What is JIT (Just-in-Time) provisioning in SAML context?
o: Creating user accounts in the SP automatically on first successful SAML login
exp: JIT provisioning uses SAML assertion attributes to create or update the SP-side user record automatically.

q: What is the AudienceRestriction element in a SAML assertion?
o: Limits the assertion to be valid only for a specific SP's EntityID
Restricts assertion to a time window
Limits which users can receive it
exp: The SP must verify the AudienceRestriction matches its own EntityID to prevent assertion recipient spoofing.

q: What version of SAML is most widely deployed today?
exp: SAML 2.0 (2005) consolidated prior versions and is the universal enterprise SSO standard.

q: How does SAML compare to OpenID Connect for mobile apps?
o: OIDC is preferred for mobile — it uses JSON/JWT and is simpler; SAML is XML-heavy
exp: OIDC's lightweight JSON tokens and REST-friendly design make it the better choice for mobile and single-page apps.

q: What is the SubjectConfirmationMethod in SAML?
o: How the SP verifies the assertion subject — typically bearer (urn:...bearer)
exp: Bearer confirmation means whoever presents the assertion is assumed to be the stated subject — relying on TLS for transport security.

q: What is OAuth 2.0?
o: An authorisation framework enabling third-party apps to access resources on a user's behalf
exp: OAuth 2.0 (RFC 6749) delegates authorisation without sharing passwords — users consent to scoped access.

q: What is an access token in OAuth 2.0?
o: A credential representing delegated permission to access a protected resource
exp: Access tokens are short-lived credentials that resource servers validate to authorise API calls.

q: What is the Authorization Code flow in OAuth 2.0?
o: Auth code is exchanged for tokens server-side — most secure for server apps
Tokens returned directly in the redirect URL
exp: The auth code is a short-lived intermediary exchanged at the token endpoint, keeping tokens out of the browser.

q: What is PKCE and why is it used?
o: Proof Key for Code Exchange — prevents auth code interception in public clients
exp: PKCE adds a code_verifier/code_challenge to the auth code flow, protecting mobile and SPA apps against code interception.

q: What is a refresh token?
o: A long-lived token used to obtain new access tokens without re-authenticating
exp: Refresh tokens should be stored securely and rotated on use to limit the impact of compromise.

q: What is OAuth scope?
o: A parameter defining what permissions the access token grants
exp: Scopes like read:email or write:contacts let users grant limited permissions to third-party apps.

q: What is the Client Credentials grant?
o: Machine-to-machine OAuth flow — client authenticates directly with no user involved
exp: Used for backend services authenticating with their own credentials rather than on behalf of a user.

q: What is the difference between OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect?
o: OAuth 2.0 handles authorisation; OIDC adds authentication via the ID token
exp: OIDC is a thin identity layer on OAuth 2.0 — it returns an ID token (JWT) proving who the user is.

q: What is an OAuth token substitution attack?
o: Using a token issued for one service at a different resource server
exp: Audience (aud) claim validation in access tokens prevents tokens intended for Service A from being used at Service B.

q: What is the Device Authorization Grant?
o: Allows devices with no browser (smart TVs, CLIs) to authenticate by displaying a code
OAuth for IoT sensors without display
exp: The device displays a user_code; the user authenticates on a separate browser and the device polls for the token.

q: What is OAuth token introspection (RFC 7662)?
o: A server-side endpoint that validates a token and returns its metadata
exp: Introspection lets resource servers validate opaque tokens by querying the authorisation server rather than needing JWT signing keys.

q: What is the implicit flow in OAuth 2.0 and why is it deprecated?
o: Tokens returned directly in redirect URI fragment — deprecated due to token leakage risk
exp: OAuth Security BCP (RFC 9700) deprecates implicit flow in favour of auth code + PKCE for all public clients.

q: What is token rotation in OAuth?
o: Issuing a new refresh token on every use and invalidating the old one
exp: Refresh token rotation limits the lifespan of any stolen refresh token and enables detection of replay attacks.

q: What is a JWT (JSON Web Token) structure?
o: Header.Payload.Signature — three Base64url-encoded parts separated by dots
An XML structure encoded as JSON
exp: The header declares the algorithm, the payload carries claims, and the signature verifies integrity.

q: What is the audience (aud) claim in a JWT?
o: The intended recipient(s) of the token — the resource server must verify it matches
exp: If a resource server doesn't validate aud, it may accept tokens intended for other services.

q: What does the 'state' parameter in OAuth prevent?
o: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) attacks during the authorisation redirect
exp: The client generates a random state value; the authorisation server echoes it back; the client verifies it.

q: What is the difference between a confidential and public OAuth client?
o: Confidential clients can store a client_secret; public clients (mobile/SPA) cannot
exp: Public clients must use PKCE or other mechanisms since they can't keep secrets; confidential clients authenticate with client credentials.

q: What is OAuth token revocation (RFC 7009)?
o: An endpoint allowing clients to invalidate access or refresh tokens
exp: Token revocation is critical for logout and incident response — revoking a refresh token stops further access token issuance.

q: What is an OAuth 2.0 authorisation server?
o: The server that authenticates the user and issues access tokens
exp: The authorisation server (Okta, Entra ID, Auth0) manages consent, token issuance, and token lifecycle.

q: What is DPoP (Demonstrating Proof of Possession) in OAuth?
o: A mechanism binding tokens to a specific client key pair, preventing token theft reuse
exp: DPoP requires the client to sign each request with a private key, making stolen tokens useless without the key.

q: What does SCIM stand for?
exp: SCIM (RFC 7642-7644) standardises user and group provisioning between identity providers and cloud apps.

q: What problem does SCIM solve?
o: Automating user provisioning and deprovisioning across multiple applications
exp: Without SCIM, each app requires custom provisioning APIs; SCIM provides a standard REST interface for account lifecycle management.

q: What HTTP methods does SCIM primarily use?
o: GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE — standard REST operations
exp: SCIM uses REST over HTTPS, making it easy to implement and debug compared to LDAP-based provisioning.

q: What are the core SCIM resource types?
exp: SCIM 2.0 defines User and Group as the core schema types, with enterprise extensions for attributes like manager and department.

q: What is SCIM provisioning vs deprovisioning?
o: Provisioning creates/updates accounts; deprovisioning disables/deletes them on offboarding
Provisioning is manual; deprovisioning is automated
exp: Timely deprovisioning (disabling accounts immediately on termination) prevents ex-employee access — SCIM automates this.

q: What is a SCIM PATCH operation used for?
o: Partially updating a resource (e.g., changing only the active status or one attribute)
exp: PATCH with JSON Patch operations allows efficient incremental updates without sending the full user object.

q: What is SCIM's schema extension mechanism?
o: Enterprise user extension (urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:enterprise:2.0:User) adding attributes like costCenter
exp: The enterprise extension adds attributes like employeeNumber, organization, manager, and division to the core User schema.

q: What does the 'active' attribute in SCIM control?
o: Whether the user account is enabled or disabled in the target application
exp: Setting active=false triggers deprovisioning in the SP, disabling the account without deleting it.

q: What is SCIM bulk operation?
o: A single HTTP request performing multiple provisioning operations for efficiency
exp: SCIM bulk (RFC 7644) reduces round trips when provisioning or updating many users simultaneously.

q: What is the difference between push provisioning and pull provisioning in SCIM?
o: Push: IdP sends changes to SP in real time; Pull: SP queries IdP periodically
Push is only for group sync
exp: Push provisioning (IdP-initiated SCIM requests) is near-real-time; pull (SP polls IdP) introduces latency.

q: What authentication mechanism does SCIM typically use?
o: OAuth 2.0 Bearer tokens for SCIM API calls
exp: SCIM APIs are protected by OAuth Bearer tokens — the IdP (Okta, Entra) exchanges a token to provision the SP.

q: What is SCIM's /ServiceProviderConfig endpoint?
o: Returns the SP's SCIM capabilities — supported operations, authentication schemes, bulk support
exp: IdPs query this endpoint to auto-configure their SCIM provisioning based on the SP's declared capabilities.

q: What is the externalId attribute in SCIM?
o: An identifier from the provisioning source (IdP) stored on the SP to correlate records
exp: externalId enables the SP to reconcile SCIM records with the source identity, handling renames and re-provisioning.

q: What is SCIM filtering?
o: Query parameter (filter=userName eq 'user@example.com') to retrieve specific resources
A firewall rule on SCIM traffic
exp: SCIM filtering allows IdPs to check if a user already exists before creating them, preventing duplicate accounts.

q: Which major IdPs support SCIM provisioning?
o: Okta, Microsoft Entra ID, Google Workspace, and many others
exp: SCIM is broadly supported by all major cloud IdPs and hundreds of SaaS applications in their app catalogues.

q: What is the SCIM /Schemas endpoint used for?
o: Discovering all supported schema definitions and their attributes
exp: Clients query /Schemas to understand attribute names, data types, and mutability for dynamic integration configuration.

q: What is SCIM entitlement provisioning?
o: Assigning application-specific roles or permissions (entitlements) during user provisioning
exp: Entitlements extend SCIM beyond account creation to assign roles like 'Sales Manager' or 'read-only' in the target app.

q: Why is SCIM preferable to LDAP for cloud app provisioning?
o: SCIM uses REST/HTTPS — firewall-friendly and cloud-native vs LDAP's binary protocol on port 389
exp: SCIM works natively over HTTPS without needing open inbound firewall ports, making it ideal for SaaS provisioning.

q: What is SCIM's PUT vs PATCH for updates?
o: PUT replaces the entire resource; PATCH applies partial updates to specific attributes
PUT is incremental; PATCH is full replace
exp: PATCH is preferred for efficiency — only changed attributes are sent rather than the full user object.

q: What is SCIM's role in a JML (Joiner/Mover/Leaver) process?
o: Automates account creation on join, attribute updates on role change, and disable/delete on leave
Manual trigger required for each stage
exp: SCIM-driven JML ensures access is granted and revoked accurately and immediately, reducing risk from stale accounts.

q: What does MFA stand for?
exp: MFA requires two or more distinct factors — dramatically reducing account takeover risk vs passwords alone.

q: What is TOTP?
o: Time-based One-Time Password — 6-digit code regenerating every 30 seconds
exp: TOTP (RFC 6238) uses a shared secret + current Unix time to generate codes, used by Google Authenticator and Authy.

q: What is FIDO2/WebAuthn?
o: W3C standard for passwordless/phishing-resistant auth using device-bound public key cryptography
exp: WebAuthn allows browsers to use device biometrics or hardware keys for strong phishing-resistant authentication.

q: Why is SMS OTP considered weaker than authenticator app TOTP?
o: SIM swapping and SS7 attacks can intercept SMS codes; TOTP is device-bound
TOTP codes can be intercepted by telcos
exp: NIST SP 800-63B deems SMS OTP 'restricted' due to real-world SIM swap attacks against high-value targets.

q: What is a push notification MFA?
o: Auth app receives a push asking 'Approve or Deny?' — user taps to confirm
exp: Push MFA (Okta Verify, Duo Push) is user-friendly but vulnerable to MFA fatigue/push bombing attacks.

q: What is an MFA fatigue (push bombing) attack?
o: Spamming a user with push notifications hoping they approve out of frustration
exp: Attackers like Lapsus$ used push bombing to compromise Uber and Microsoft — mitigated by number matching.

q: What is number matching in push MFA?
o: Displaying a number on the login screen the user must enter in the auth app to approve
exp: Number matching prevents accidental approvals and thwarts push bombing by requiring user to actively confirm the number.

q: What is HOTP?
o: HMAC-based One-Time Password — counter-based OTP (predecessor to TOTP)
exp: HOTP (RFC 4226) uses a counter rather than time; counters must stay synchronised between client and server.

q: What is a hardware security key (e.g., YubiKey)?
o: A physical USB/NFC device providing phishing-resistant FIDO2/U2F second factor
A type of smart card only
exp: Hardware keys provide the highest assurance MFA — the key verifies the site's origin, blocking phishing.

q: What is real-time phishing (adversary-in-the-middle) against MFA?
o: Proxy that relays credentials AND MFA codes in real time, capturing session cookies
exp: Tools like Evilginx2 and Modlishka relay auth in real time, capturing session cookies that bypass MFA.

q: What is step-up authentication?
o: Requiring additional auth factors when a user attempts a higher-risk action
exp: Step-up auth is triggered contextually (e.g., accessing financial settings) without requiring re-login for normal use.

q: What is the 'something you are' authentication factor?
o: Biometrics — fingerprint, face recognition, iris scan
exp: Inherence factors (biometrics) verify the user's physical characteristics rather than knowledge or possession.

q: What is passwordless authentication?
o: Authenticating without a password — using biometrics, FIDO2 keys, or magic links
exp: Passwordless eliminates the weakest link (reusable passwords) while maintaining or improving assurance levels.

q: What are NIST AAL (Authenticator Assurance Levels)?
o: AAL1 (single factor), AAL2 (MFA), AAL3 (hardware-bound MFA) — from NIST SP 800-63B
exp: AAL3 requires hardware cryptographic authenticators (FIDO2 keys), the highest assurance in NIST 800-63B.

q: What is a magic link?
o: One-time URL sent via email that authenticates the user on click
exp: Magic links provide passwordless login; security depends on the email account's security and link expiry time.

q: What is risk-based authentication (RBA)?
o: Adjusting authentication requirements dynamically based on risk signals at login time
exp: RBA evaluates device posture, location, velocity, and behaviour to challenge only anomalous logins.

q: What is a soft token?
o: A software-based OTP generator on a smartphone (e.g., Google Authenticator)
exp: Soft tokens generate TOTP codes in apps and are more convenient but less secure than hardware tokens.

q: What is FIDO U2F vs FIDO2?
o: U2F is the older second-factor-only standard; FIDO2 adds passwordless authentication via WebAuthn
FIDO2 is less secure than U2F
U2F supports passwordless; FIDO2 does not
exp: FIDO2 = CTAP2 + WebAuthn; it supports both second-factor and passwordless flows, superseding U2F.

q: What is MFA bypass via account recovery?
o: Exploiting weak recovery methods (e.g., SMS reset) to circumvent strong MFA
exp: If account recovery is weaker than MFA (e.g., SMS fallback), attackers target recovery instead.

q: What is the purpose of backup codes in MFA?
o: One-time codes to regain access if the primary MFA device is lost
exp: Backup codes should be stored securely offline — they're as sensitive as passwords if intercepted.

q: What is Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD)?
o: Microsoft's cloud-based identity and access management service
exp: Entra ID provides SSO, MFA, Conditional Access, and B2B/B2C identity for Microsoft 365 and Azure workloads.

q: What is Conditional Access in Entra ID?
o: Policy engine granting/blocking access based on signals like user, device, location, and risk
exp: Conditional Access evaluates signals and enforces controls (require MFA, block, require compliant device) at access time.

q: What is Microsoft Entra Privileged Identity Management (PIM)?
o: Just-in-time activation of privileged roles with approval workflows and time limits
exp: PIM eliminates standing privilege by requiring role activation with MFA, justification, and optional approval.

q: What is Self-Service Password Reset (SSPR) in Entra?
o: Allows users to reset passwords and unlock accounts without IT helpdesk
exp: SSPR reduces helpdesk tickets and improves security by requiring multiple verification methods before reset.

q: What is Entra Connect (Azure AD Connect)?
o: Tool synchronising on-premises AD identities to Entra ID for hybrid identity
A direct AD to Azure VPN
exp: Entra Connect (now Entra Connect Sync / Cloud Sync) bridges on-prem AD and Entra ID for hybrid environments.

q: What is a Managed Identity in Azure?
o: An automatically managed service identity in Entra ID used by Azure resources without storing credentials
A human identity managed by IT
A service account with a password
An API key for Azure services
exp: Managed identities (system-assigned and user-assigned) eliminate the need for secrets in code accessing Azure services.

q: What is Entra ID Protection?
o: Risk-based identity protection detecting compromised credentials and risky sign-ins
exp: Entra ID Protection uses machine learning to detect sign-in risk (anonymous IP, impossible travel) and user risk (leaked credentials).

q: What is an Entra ID App Registration?
o: Registering an application to receive tokens and access Entra-protected resources
Installing an app on Azure VMs
exp: App registrations define the app's identity, redirect URIs, API permissions, and certificate/secret credentials.

q: What is Entra External Identities (B2B)?
o: Enabling guest users from external organisations to access resources using their own credentials
exp: Entra B2B invites external users to collaborate without creating separate accounts — they authenticate with their own IdP.

q: What is the difference between Entra ID Free, P1, and P2?
o: P1 adds Conditional Access and hybrid identity; P2 adds Identity Protection, PIM, and access reviews
P1 is for consumers; P2 for enterprise
exp: P2 (EMS E3/E5) unlocks risk-based Conditional Access, PIM, Identity Governance, and automated access reviews.

q: What is Microsoft Entra Verified ID?
o: Decentralised identity solution for issuing and verifying tamper-proof digital credentials
exp: Verified ID uses W3C Verifiable Credentials and DIDs — users hold credentials in a wallet and share selective attributes.

q: What is an Entra Access Review?
o: Periodic review of user access rights to ensure continued appropriateness
exp: Access reviews automate the recertification of user memberships and app assignments, supporting least-privilege.

q: What is Entra Workload Identity Federation?
o: Allowing external workloads (GitHub Actions, AWS) to access Azure resources without secrets using OIDC tokens
exp: Workload identity federation replaces long-lived secrets with short-lived OIDC tokens from trusted external IdPs.

q: What is a service principal in Entra ID?
o: The identity an application or service uses to access Entra-protected resources
exp: Service principals are app instances of App Registrations — each tenant where an app is consented gets its own service principal.

q: What is the Microsoft Authenticator app's number matching feature?
o: Requires users to enter a number shown on login screen in the app, defeating push bombing
Matches user's phone number to account
exp: Microsoft enabled number matching by default in 2023 to combat MFA fatigue attacks targeting Entra ID accounts.

q: What is Entra ID's Primary Refresh Token (PRT)?
o: A long-lived token on Windows devices enabling seamless SSO across apps without re-prompting
exp: PRTs are device-bound cryptographic tokens; PRT theft (via token theft malware) allows persistent access bypassing MFA.

q: What is Entra Global Secure Access?
o: Microsoft's Security Service Edge (SSE) product providing Zero Trust Network Access via Entra
exp: GSA (Microsoft Entra Internet/Private Access) routes traffic through Microsoft's network with Conditional Access enforcement.

q: What is the Global Administrator role in Entra ID?
o: The highest-privilege role with full control over the Entra tenant
An admin for Azure VMs only
exp: Global Admins should be minimised and protected with hardware MFA and PIM — they can take over any resource.

q: What is Entra ID's sign-in log?
o: Audit log recording all authentication events, outcomes, and applied Conditional Access policies
exp: Sign-in logs feed into Microsoft Sentinel and third-party SIEMs for security monitoring and compliance reporting.

q: What is Cross-Tenant Synchronisation in Entra?
o: Automatically syncing users from one Entra tenant to another for multi-tenant collaboration
exp: Cross-tenant sync extends Entra Connect Sync to federate identities across multiple owned Entra tenants.

q: What does PAM stand for in IAM?
o: Privileged Access Management — controlling access to critical systems by privileged accounts
exp: PAM secures, monitors, and audits privileged accounts (root, admin, service accounts) that pose the greatest risk.

q: What is credential vaulting?
o: Securely storing privileged credentials in an encrypted vault that brokers access
exp: PAM vaults (CyberArk, BeyondTrust, Delinea) check out credentials on demand without exposing them to users.

q: What is session recording in PAM?
o: Recording and storing privileged sessions for audit, forensics, and compliance
exp: Session recordings provide an irrefutable audit trail of privileged actions, essential for compliance and investigations.

q: What is just-in-time (JIT) privileged access?
o: Granting elevated access only when needed for a specific task, auto-revoking afterwards
exp: JIT reduces the standing privilege attack surface — no persistent admin accounts that can be harvested.

q: What are service accounts in the context of PAM?
o: Non-human accounts used by applications, scripts, and services to access resources
exp: Service accounts often have high privileges and never-expiring passwords — key targets for attackers.

q: What is password rotation in PAM?
o: Automatically changing privileged credentials after each use or on a schedule
exp: Automatic rotation ensures compromised credentials are short-lived and reduces the impact of credential exposure.

q: What is the principle of least privilege as applied in PAM?
o: Accounts should have only the minimum rights needed — no permanent standing admin access
All admins get full access for efficiency
Least privilege is for users only
exp: PAM enforces least privilege for privileged accounts specifically, where the risk is highest.

q: What is SSH key management in PAM?
o: Discovering, rotating, and controlling SSH keys used for privileged access to servers
exp: Unmanaged SSH keys are a major PAM risk — attackers can move laterally with stolen or forgotten private keys.

q: What is a break-glass account?
o: Emergency high-privilege account sealed and monitored for crisis use only
exp: Break-glass accounts are vaulted, monitored, and trigger immediate alerts on access — a last resort for emergencies.

q: What is EPM (Endpoint Privilege Management)?
o: Removing local admin rights from endpoints and elevating only specific applications on demand
exp: EPM (CyberArk EPM, BeyondTrust PowerBroker) lets users run specific apps elevated without full admin, reducing attack surface.

q: What is dual control / four-eyes principle in PAM?
o: Requiring two authorised individuals to approve and perform sensitive privileged actions
exp: Dual control prevents insider threats and errors by requiring two parties for high-impact privileged operations.

q: What is the risk of shared privileged accounts?
o: No accountability — actions can't be attributed to an individual; hard to rotate credentials
Shared accounts are recommended for admin tasks
exp: Shared admin accounts (admin, root) are a major audit and security concern — PAM eliminates or wraps them.

q: What is secrets management as part of PAM?
o: Storing and managing application secrets (API keys, certificates, DB passwords) in a secrets vault
exp: Tools like HashiCorp Vault and AWS Secrets Manager centralise and automate rotation of application credentials.

q: What is the PAM discovery phase?
o: Scanning the environment to find all privileged accounts, credentials, and entitlements
exp: You can't protect what you can't see — discovery identifies shadow admins, unmanaged service accounts, and SSH keys.

q: What is command filtering in PAM?
o: Allowing privileged sessions to run only approved commands, blocking dangerous ones
exp: Command filtering (CyberArk) enforces least privilege within a privileged session, preventing destructive commands.

q: What is a PAM audit trail?
o: Immutable record of who accessed what privileged account, when, and what actions were taken
exp: PAM audit trails satisfy compliance requirements (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, SOX) for privileged user monitoring.

q: What is identity threat detection and response (ITDR)?
o: Detecting and responding to attacks targeting identity infrastructure (AD, IdP, PAM)
exp: ITDR (CrowdStrike Identity Protection, Vectra) focuses on identity-based attack detection like Golden Tickets and DCSync.

q: What is privileged account lifecycle management?
o: Governing the creation, review, and decommissioning of privileged accounts
exp: Orphaned privileged accounts are a top risk — lifecycle management ensures they're reviewed and removed promptly.

q: What is lateral movement via privileged credentials?
o: Using harvested admin credentials to move between systems after initial compromise
exp: PAM limits lateral movement by rotating credentials, isolating sessions, and requiring JIT elevation per target.

q: What is a PAM policy?
o: Rules defining who can access which privileged resources, under what conditions, and with what controls
exp: PAM policies encode least privilege, dual control, session recording, and approval workflows per resource type.

q: What does RBAC stand for?
o: Role-Based Access Control — permissions assigned via roles rather than directly to users
exp: RBAC simplifies permissions management by grouping entitlements into roles that are assigned to users.

q: What is the principle of least privilege?
o: Granting only the minimum access rights necessary for a user to perform their job
Granting access to all resources for convenience
exp: Least privilege limits the blast radius if an account is compromised or misused.

q: What is Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)?
o: Access decisions based on attributes of users, resources, and environment rather than roles
A more complex version of RBAC
exp: ABAC enables fine-grained, context-aware policies like 'allow if user.department=Finance AND resource.classification=Confidential.'

q: What is role explosion in RBAC?
o: Proliferation of too many granular roles making the model unmanageable
A DDoS on the IAM system
exp: Role explosion occurs when teams create unique roles per use case rather than reusing composable roles.

q: What is separation of duties (SoD) in RBAC?
o: Ensuring no single user has permissions to complete a sensitive end-to-end process alone
A two-person rule for approvals only
exp: SoD prevents fraud — e.g., the same person shouldn't be able to both create and approve financial transactions.

q: What is a permission in RBAC?
o: The right to perform a specific action on a specific resource
exp: Permissions are atomic: create, read, update, delete on a given resource type — combined into roles.

q: What is hierarchical RBAC?
o: Roles inherit permissions from parent roles, enabling reuse and reducing redundancy
Users can have only one role
Roles change based on time of day
exp: A 'Manager' role can inherit all 'Employee' permissions plus additional management permissions.

q: What is policy-based access control (PBAC)?
o: Combining RBAC and ABAC using rich policy expressions evaluated at runtime
exp: PBAC (used in OPA, Cedar) evaluates complex policies combining role, attributes, resource, and environmental context.

q: What are orphaned entitlements?
o: Access permissions that remain after the business justification has ended (e.g., after role change)
exp: Orphaned entitlements accumulate via 'access creep' — mitigated by access reviews and automated deprovisioning.

q: What is an access review (recertification)?
o: Periodic process where managers certify that their team's access remains appropriate
A penetration test of access controls
exp: Regular recertification (quarterly, annually) is required by PCI-DSS, SOX, and ISO 27001 for privileged access.

q: What is the difference between authorisation and authentication?
o: Authentication verifies who you are; authorisation determines what you can do
exp: RBAC governs authorisation — what authenticated users are permitted to do after their identity is confirmed.

q: What is a default-deny access control model?
o: All access is denied unless explicitly permitted by a policy
Access is allowed unless explicitly denied
Access depends on time of day
exp: Default-deny (whitelist) is more secure than default-allow (blacklist) because unknown access is rejected.

q: What is ReBAC (Relationship-Based Access Control)?
o: Access decisions based on relationships between subjects and objects (e.g., owner, member of team)
exp: ReBAC (used in Google Zanzibar, OpenFGA) models access like 'user can edit if user is member of doc's parent folder.'

q: What is privileged role in RBAC context?
o: A role with elevated permissions to sensitive resources requiring extra governance
Any role with more than 5 permissions
exp: Privileged roles (admin, superuser, DBA) should be governed with JIT, approval workflows, and access reviews.

q: What is access provisioning?
o: The process of granting users the access they need, typically on onboarding or role change
exp: Provisioning should be driven by role/job function and automated via IAM workflows to ensure accuracy and speed.

q: What is toxic combination in RBAC/SoD?
o: A pair of permissions that together enable fraud or high risk (e.g., create payment + approve payment)
A role with too many permissions
Two users with the same role
exp: SoD controls detect and prevent toxic combinations — a key requirement for SOX and financial system compliance.

q: What is XACML?
o: eXtensible Access Control Markup Language — a standard for expressing and evaluating access policies
exp: XACML (OASIS) defines a policy language and decision/enforcement architecture for fine-grained access control.

q: What is access creep?
o: Accumulation of excessive permissions over time as users change roles without losing old access
exp: Access creep is common in organisations without automated deprovisioning or regular recertification campaigns.

q: What are static vs dynamic roles?
o: Static roles are pre-defined; dynamic roles are assigned based on real-time context/attributes
Static roles only exist in cloud systems
exp: Dynamic roles (e.g., 'on-call engineer') can be assigned and revoked automatically based on context.

q: What is an entitlement catalogue?
o: A centralised inventory of all available permissions and roles users can request
exp: Entitlement catalogues power self-service access request portals, giving users visibility into available roles.

q: What is Single Sign-On (SSO)?
o: Authentication mechanism allowing users to log in once and access multiple applications
exp: SSO reduces credential fatigue, improves UX, and centralises authentication to a single, well-secured IdP.

q: What protocols are commonly used to implement SSO?
exp: SAML is dominant for enterprise web SSO; OIDC for modern apps; Kerberos for Windows integrated auth.

q: What is federated identity in the context of SSO?
o: Trusting an external IdP's authentication assertion to grant access across organisational boundaries
exp: Federation enables cross-organisational SSO — Company A's users can access Company B's resources via established trust.

q: What is the difference between SSO and password synchronisation?
o: SSO authenticates once at the IdP; password sync copies passwords to each app — fundamentally different
exp: SSO eliminates passwords being sent to each app; password sync still distributes credentials, increasing exposure.

q: What is a session cookie in SSO?
o: A cookie storing the session token that proves the user is authenticated at the IdP or SP
A password stored in the browser
A SAML assertion stored as a cookie
An OIDC ID token stored in a cookie
exp: SSO session cookies enable transparent re-authentication across apps within the same browser session.

q: What is Single Logout (SLO) and why is it challenging?
o: Terminating sessions at the IdP and all SPs simultaneously — challenging because SPs may not implement it
Logging out of one app only
exp: SLO is hard because each SP must handle the logout request; many implement it poorly or not at all.

q: What is Kerberos-based SSO?
o: Windows-integrated SSO using Kerberos tickets — users are silently authenticated to intranet apps
exp: IWA (Integrated Windows Authentication) uses Kerberos tickets from AD login to silently authenticate to intranet apps.

q: What is the security risk of a centralised SSO IdP?
o: If the IdP is compromised, all connected applications are potentially compromised
SSO makes the system less efficient
The IdP must store all passwords
exp: SSO concentrates authentication risk — strong MFA, PAM, and monitoring of the IdP are critical.

q: What is token hijacking in SSO?
o: Stealing a valid SSO session token or assertion to impersonate the user across all connected apps
CSRF on the IdP login page
exp: A stolen SAML assertion or OIDC access token may grant access to all apps in the SSO ecosystem — binding tokens to devices mitigates this.

q: What is deep link SSO (resource URL access)?
o: User clicks a direct URL to a protected resource and is redirected to IdP for auth, then back
User must always start at IdP portal
exp: SP-initiated SSO supports deep linking — the original URL is preserved in the relay state and the user lands there after auth.

q: What is IdP discovery?
o: Mechanism for an SP to determine which IdP to redirect the user to in multi-IdP environments
exp: IdP discovery uses email domain hints, hosted domain parameters, or cookies to route users to their correct IdP.

q: What is social login?
o: Using credentials from a social provider (Google, Apple, GitHub) to authenticate to a third-party app via OIDC
A less secure form of SSO
exp: Social login (implemented with OIDC) lets users avoid creating new accounts — convenience vs. privacy tradeoffs apply.

q: What is headless SSO?
o: Machine-to-machine SSO where automated processes authenticate without a human browser interaction
exp: Headless SSO is used in RPA, CI/CD, and API integrations — typically uses client credentials or service tokens.

q: What is the relay state in SAML SSO?
o: An opaque parameter preserving the destination URL through the IdP redirect so users land at the right page
exp: The SP encodes the target URL in the relay state; the IdP returns it unchanged so the SP can redirect the user correctly.

q: What is Enterprise SSO vs Consumer SSO?
o: Enterprise SSO (SAML/OIDC to SaaS apps) targets employees; Consumer SSO (social login, OIDC) targets end-users
exp: B2E IAM optimises for security and auditability; B2C IAM optimises for user experience and scale.

q: What is the open redirect vulnerability in SSO flows?
o: Attacker manipulates the redirect_uri to send auth codes/tokens to a malicious endpoint
exp: OAuth/OIDC servers must strictly validate redirect_uri against pre-registered values to prevent token leakage.

q: What is cross-domain SSO?
o: Enabling SSO across applications on different domains (e.g., app.acme.com and app.partner.com)
SSO within a single domain only
exp: Cross-domain SSO typically uses SAML or OIDC redirects since cookies don't cross domains.

q: What is an SSO portal (app launcher)?
o: A central page showing all apps a user can access via SSO — like Okta's or Entra's My Apps portal
exp: The SSO portal is the user's starting point for accessing all provisioned apps without separate logins.

q: What is continuous authentication in the context of SSO?
o: Ongoing monitoring of session signals (behaviour, device, location) to re-evaluate trust during a session
exp: Continuous authentication detects anomalies mid-session and can trigger step-up or session termination without full re-login.

q: What is the impact of IdP downtime on SSO-reliant applications?
o: All SSO-dependent apps become inaccessible — making IdP HA and redundancy critical
Users fall back to local passwords automatically
No impact if apps cache tokens
exp: SSO concentrates availability risk — IdP redundancy (multi-region, active-active) is critical for business continuity.
